race.name: Nove Colli
race.tagline: The world's oldest gran fondo. Nine hills. 200 km. Pantani's hometown. Since 1971.

vitals.registration: Online via novecolli.it. Registration opens in autumn. Entry: EUR99-110. The annual jersey design is a collector's item — included with registration.
vitals.prize_purse: None (timed event with age-group rankings, finisher medals)
vitals.aid_stations: Feed zones at strategic points between hills — Italian hospitality standard. Bananas, crostata, energy food, water, cola. The Nove Colli feed zones reflect Italian cycling culture: generous and warm.
vitals.cutoff_time: Broom wagon enforced. Time limits at intermediate checkpoints.
distance_options: Gran Fondo: 200 km (124 mi), 3,814 m gain — 9 hills (the full Nove Colli)
Medio Fondo: 130 km (81 mi), ~2,400 m gain — selected hills

climate.primary: Late May Emilia-Romagna — pleasant but unpredictable
climate.description: Late May in Emilia-Romagna is generally pleasant: temperatures range from 13-21C (55-70F), with warm afternoons and cool morning starts. However, May is the rainiest month in the region (averaging 68mm across 16 days), and afternoon thunderstorms over the hills can materialize quickly. The 6:00 AM start is cool. The afternoon finish in Cesenatico can be warm. Not the extreme conditions of Alpine events, but rain is a genuine possibility.
challenges: May is the rainiest month — significant probability of wet roads
Steep descents (up to 18% gradients) on wet roads are treacherous
Early morning start (6:00 AM) can be cold — 13-15C at the Adriatic coast
Afternoon warmth (20-25C) on the later hills if conditions are clear

terrain.primary: Paved Italian provincial roads through hilly countryside
terrain.surface: Italian provincial roads — well-maintained tarmac through the Emilia-Romagna hills. Road quality is generally good, with some rougher sections on minor hill roads. All paved throughout. The terrain is rolling to punchy — short, steep hills rather than long mountain passes.
features: Barbotto (372m in 5.5 km, max 18%) — the signature hill. Short, vicious, with gradients that demand low gearing and strong legs
Monte Pugliano (511m in 9 km) — the longest climb with the most elevation gain
Polenta — steep approach through classic Romagnol countryside
Pieve di Rivoschio — punchy hill with tight corners
Montetiffi — exposed hillside climb with panoramic views
Perticara — sustained effort through mining village
Siepi — penultimate hill, punchy and draining
Gorolo — the final hill before the descent back to Cesenatico and the Adriatic

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: The world's oldest gran fondo, born in 1971 with 17 riders in Marco Pantani's hometown. 55 years of continuous operation. The name means 'Nine Hills' — the route's defining identity. prestige=4 earns a one-tier promotion but cannot promote T2 into T1 — remains Tier 2.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: cultural_impact=4 for the world's oldest GF (1971), Pantani's hometown, cycling club named after Fausto Coppi. prestige=4 (the original gran fondo, deeply respected in Italian cycling, but lacks the global Monument status of Paris-Roubaix or L'Etape's Tour de France brand). altitude=1 — the hills top out below 600m. climate=2 — May rain is likely but conditions are temperate, not extreme. p4 promotion does not change T2 placement.

logistics.explanation: Cesenatico is well-connected: Rimini airport is 25 minutes away, Bologna is 1.5 hours, and both have good international connections. The Adriatic coast has abundant accommodation at reasonable prices. The start and finish are in the same location (Cesenatico port), eliminating the transfer logistics that plague point-to-point events. 55 years of organizational experience means packet pickup, parking, and race-day logistics are well-rehearsed. The flat Adriatic setting makes getting around easy.

length.explanation: 200 km (124 miles) for the Gran Fondo is a serious day — the distance alone puts it among the longest gran fondos in Europe. Combined with 3,814m of climbing across 9 hills, most riders finish in 7-10 hours. The Medio Fondo (130 km) offers a shorter alternative. The distance is demanding but not ultra — the challenge comes from the repeated short, steep climbs rather than the pure kilometer count.

technicality.explanation: All paved throughout, but the steep gradients (up to 18% on Barbotto) create a form of technicality that flat-road riders don't experience. Holding a line on an 18% gradient, especially on tired legs and especially in rain, requires bike control. The descents from the steeper hills are also technical — tight corners, narrow roads, limited visibility. Not cobblestone-level technicality, but not pure endurance either.

elevation.explanation: 3,814 meters of climbing across 9 named hills. None of the hills are long — the longest (Monte Pugliano) is 9 km — but they are steep and relentless. Barbotto's 18% maximum gradient is as steep as anything in the Tour de France. The cumulative effect of 9 punchy climbs over 200 km creates a significant climbing workload. Not Alpine scale, but genuinely demanding.

climate.explanation: Late May on the Adriatic coast of Italy is generally pleasant — 13-21C, long daylight hours, warm afternoons. Not the extreme Alpine conditions that earn high climate scores. The risk is rain: May is the wettest month in the region, and wet 18% gradients on Italian provincial roads are no joke. But compared to the Galibier at 2,642m or wet cobblestones in April, the climate challenge here is moderate.

altitude.explanation: The highest point on the route is approximately 600m above sea level. Altitude is completely irrelevant. These are hills, not mountains. The Emilia-Romagna interior rises gently from the Adriatic plain into the pre-Apennine foothills. Oxygen levels are identical to sea level.

adventure.explanation: The Emilia-Romagna countryside is charming — rolling green hills, small stone villages, vineyards, and olive groves. Cesenatico's canal port (designed by Leonardo da Vinci) is genuinely beautiful. But this is not the Dolomites or the cobblestones of northern France. The scenery is pleasant, not jaw-dropping. The adventure is cultural — riding through Pantani's homeland, past the espresso bars and trattorias of the Romagna — rather than geographical.

prestige.explanation: The world's oldest gran fondo, period. Founded in 1971 — a decade before La Marmotte, two decades before L'Etape. The cycling club that created it is named after Fausto Coppi. The start town is Marco Pantani's birthplace. Fifty-five years of continuous operation make Nove Colli a foundational event in the history of amateur cycling. In Italian cycling culture, it is deeply revered. Globally, it lacks the Monument status of Paris-Roubaix or the Tour de France brand of L'Etape, but those who know cycling history know Nove Colli.

race_quality.explanation: 55 years of organizational refinement from a passionate local cycling club. The event is timed with age-group rankings, providing competitive structure. The annual collector's jersey — a different design each year — has become a cycling tradition. Feed zones are well-stocked with Italian hospitality. Road closures on the hilly sections are well-managed. Medical support and broom wagon coverage are reliable. Not quite ASO-level production, but deeply competent and infused with genuine cycling passion.

experience.explanation: Starting from Cesenatico's canal port at dawn — the port designed by Leonardo da Vinci — sets the tone. The nine hills each have distinct character: Barbotto's savage 18% gradients, Monte Pugliano's long grind, Gorolo's final sting. The rolling Romagnol countryside between hills is genuinely beautiful — vineyards, stone walls, small villages. The return to Cesenatico, descending from the last hill toward the Adriatic, is a warm reward. Not the transcendence of Alpine summits, but a deeply satisfying Italian cycling day.

community.explanation: 10,000 riders create a festive atmosphere that draws on Italian cycling's inherent warmth. The Romagna is passionate about cycling — Pantani's memory is honored throughout the event. The pre-race pasta party, the post-ride celebrations in the Cesenatico port area, and the collector's jersey tradition create a community that extends beyond race day. Many riders return annually — the jersey collection becomes a cycling archive. The field is predominantly Italian but with a growing international contingent.

field_depth.explanation: Italian club riders push hard at the front — age-group competition is taken seriously in Italian gran fondo culture. The steep gradients reward pure climbing ability, and the front of the race moves fast. But with 10,000 riders, the field spans from competitive to recreational. The depth is genuine in the Italian context — less so internationally. If you want to race, the sharp end is sharp. If you want to ride, there's room.

value.explanation: EUR99-110 including a collector's edition jersey for 200 km across 9 hills with full organizational support is strong value. The jersey alone — a unique annual design that has become a cycling collectible over 55 editions — is worth EUR40-60. Cesenatico's Adriatic location keeps accommodation and food costs reasonable by Italian standards. The cost-to-experience ratio is among the best in European gran fondos.

expenses.explanation: Cesenatico is an Adriatic beach town — not cheap in May but not Alpine resort pricing. Hotels run EUR80-150/night. Rimini airport (25 min) has budget airline connections across Europe. Bologna (1.5h) offers wider international connections. Italian food is outstanding and reasonably priced. Budget EUR800-1,500 from Europe, EUR1,200-2,000 from the US for a 3-5 day trip that can easily combine with Adriatic beach time.

biased_opinion.summary: Nove Colli is where the gran fondo was born. In 1971, 17 riders set out from Cesenatico — Marco Pantani's hometown, cycling club named after Fausto Coppi — to ride nine hills in the Emilia-Romagna countryside. Fifty-five years later, 10,000 riders do the same. The hills are not Alpine monsters — they are short, steep, punchy climbs that reward explosive power and punish weak legs. Barbotto at 18% is as steep as anything in the Tour de France. The route is 200 km of rolling Italian countryside, stone villages, vineyards, and espresso stops. It is not the most spectacular event in Europe, not the hardest, and not the most prestigious. It is simply the original.
strengths: The world's oldest gran fondo: continuous operation since 1971, 55th edition in 2026
Barbotto at 18% — short, savage, and as steep as any Tour de France ramp
Cesenatico: Pantani's hometown, Leonardo's canal port, Adriatic charm
Annual collector's jersey — 55 editions of cycling art history
weaknesses: Not Alpine grandeur — the hills top out below 600m with pleasant rather than spectacular scenery
May rain is a genuine probability — 68mm average across 16 rainy days
200 km is a very long day for riders who underestimate the cumulative effect of 9 hills
Limited international recognition compared to L'Etape, Maratona, or Paris-Roubaix
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Nove Colli is the gran fondo that started it all. Not the hardest, not the most famous, not the most beautiful — but the first. If you care about cycling history, if you want to ride through the homeland of Pantani and Coppi, and if nine punchy Italian hills over 200 km sounds like a proper day out, this is your event. The 55th edition jersey will look very good on your wall.

final_verdict.one_liner: The world's oldest gran fondo. Nine hills. 200 km. Pantani's hometown. Since 1971.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want to ride the original gran fondo in the homeland of Italian cycling legends. Yes if nine punchy hills with 18% gradients over 200 km of Romagnol countryside is your pace. Skip if you want Alpine spectacle — these are hills, not mountains. Skip if you need English-language support — this event is authentically Italian.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want Italian mountains: Maratona dles Dolomites (7 Dolomite passes). If you want a similar Italian GF with more climbing: Granfondo Sportful Dolomiti Race. If you want a shorter Italian sportive: Strade Bianche Gran Fondo.

logistics.airport: Rimini (RMI) — 25 minutes (closest, budget airline connections across Europe). Bologna (BLQ) — 1.5 hours (wider international connections). Forli (FRL) — 30 minutes (limited flights).
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Cesenatico — the start/finish town on the Adriatic coast. Hotels and apartments line the beach promenade. May is pre-peak season, so rates are reasonable (EUR80-150/night). Book 3-6 months in advance for event weekend. Alternatively, Rimini (25 min) offers a wider hotel selection with excellent beach-town dining.
logistics.food: Emilia-Romagna is Italy's culinary heartland. Piadina romagnola (flatbread), passatelli in brodo, tagliatelle al ragu (the original Bolognese), squacquerone cheese. Carb loading has never been this good. Cesenatico's fish restaurants on the canal port are exceptional. Feed zones serve Italian pastries and local specialties alongside standard energy food.
logistics.packet_pickup: Event village in Cesenatico, 1-2 days before. The expo combines registration, sponsor village, and the annual jersey unveiling. Well-organized for 10,000 riders.
logistics.parking: Cesenatico manages event parking in designated areas. Follow event instructions — the small town center is closed for the start. Arriving the day before and parking at your hotel is the simplest approach.

history.founder: Gruppo Sportivo Fausto Coppi Cesenatico (cycling club named after the legendary Italian champion)
history.origin_story: In 1971, the Gruppo Sportivo Fausto Coppi Cesenatico — a cycling club named after Italy's greatest champion — organized a 200 km ride over nine hills in the Emilia-Romagna countryside. Seventeen riders started. The concept — a timed, organized ride over a fixed, challenging route, open to all — was new. They called it a 'gran fondo,' and the name stuck. The event grew steadily through the 1970s and 1980s, becoming a fixture of Italian amateur cycling. By the 1990s, 'gran fondo' had become a global cycling format, and Nove Colli was its acknowledged birthplace. The connection to cycling's legends is deep: the club is named for Coppi, and the start town is the birthplace of Marco Pantani — Il Pirata, the last Italian to win the Tour de France.
notable_moments: 1971: First edition — 17 riders set out from Cesenatico over 9 hills. The gran fondo format is born.
The name 'Nove Colli' (Nine Hills) becomes the route's permanent identity
Growth from 17 riders to 10,000 — the gran fondo format spreads worldwide
Cesenatico: birthplace of Marco Pantani (1970-2004), Il Pirata, Tour de France and Giro d'Italia winner
2026: 55th continuous edition — the longest-running gran fondo in cycling history
Annual collector's jersey tradition — 55 unique designs and counting
history.reputation: The original gran fondo. Born in 1971, imitated worldwide, never replicated. Where the format that now defines amateur cycling was invented.

course_description.character: Nine punchy hills strung across 200 km of Emilia-Romagna countryside, starting and finishing at the Adriatic coast. The hills are not long — the longest is 9 km — but they are steep, with Barbotto's 18% pitches standing out as the route's signature gradient. Between hills, the terrain rolls through vineyards, olive groves, and small stone villages. The route is a loop from Cesenatico, climbing inland into the pre-Apennine foothills before descending back to the coast. The rhythm is distinctive: climb hard for 15-30 minutes, descend, roll through the valley, then climb again. Nine times.

suffering_zones.label: Barbotto — The Signature Hill
suffering_zones.desc: 372m of elevation in 5.5 km with a maximum gradient of 18%. This is the hill that defines Nove Colli. The opening ramps are steep (12-14%), then the road kicks up to 18% for a section that demands your lowest gear and your strongest legs. On 200 km-old legs in the full route, Barbotto is a wall. For many riders, this is the steepest road they'll ever ride.

suffering_zones.label: Monte Pugliano — The Longest Climb
suffering_zones.desc: 511m of elevation gain in 9 km — the longest and most sustained climb on the route. Gradients are more moderate than Barbotto (6-8% average) but the climb goes on and on. The reward at the top: views across the Emilia-Romagna hills toward the distant Apennine ridge. This is where steady climbers recoup time lost to explosive riders on steeper hills.

suffering_zones.label: Middle Hills — Polenta through Perticara
suffering_zones.desc: Five hills in succession through the heart of the Romagnol countryside. Each one is individually manageable — 3-6 km, 200-350m of climbing — but the cumulative effect across 80+ km of rolling terrain drains your reserves. The villages between hills offer brief respite and the sight of espresso bars you cannot stop at. This is the attritional middle third.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Five hills, combined ~1,500m climbing over 60 km
suffering_zones.named_section: Polenta + Pieve di Rivoschio + Ciola + Montetiffi + Perticara

suffering_zones.label: Gorolo — The Final Hill
suffering_zones.desc: The ninth and final hill before the long descent back to Cesenatico and the Adriatic. Gorolo is not the hardest climb — but it comes at km 180+, on legs that have already climbed 3,400+ meters across eight previous hills. The descent from Gorolo back toward the coast is fast and freeing — the finish in Cesenatico's port area awaits.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Final hill before the descent to Cesenatico

course_description.signature_challenge: Barbotto at 18% — the steepest sustained gradient on the route and the climb that gives Nove Colli its fearsome reputation among punchy-hill specialists. But the real challenge is the cumulative effect: nine hills over 200 km, each one requiring an effort that leaves you slightly more depleted for the next. The ninth hill is always harder than the first.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved Italian provincial roads — 9 punchy hills with gradients up to 18%
guide_variables.race_weather: Late May Emilia-Romagna — pleasant (13-21C) with significant rain probability
race_challenges: 3,814m of climbing across 9 distinct hills over 200 km
Barbotto: 18% maximum gradient — one of the steepest sustained gradients in European gran fondos
Cumulative fatigue from 9 repeated climb-descend cycles
May rain on steep descents — wet 18% gradients are treacherous

non_negotiables.requirement: Steep gradient training — repeated efforts on 12-18% grades
non_negotiables.why: Barbotto's 18% pitches demand specific steep-climbing fitness. Your normal climbing legs won't suffice — you need to be comfortable sustaining 300-400W on gradients that push your center of gravity to the tipping point. Find the steepest local climb and repeat it. Gearing: bring at minimum a 34x32.

non_negotiables.requirement: Repeated-climb endurance — 5+ distinct climbs in a single training ride
non_negotiables.why: Nine hills means nine separate climbing efforts with descents and flat sections between. Your body needs to recover on the descents and go again immediately. Train with multi-climb days: ride your local climbs in a circuit, 5-6 times, with rolling sections between.

non_negotiables.requirement: 200 km distance capability with climbing — full dress rehearsal
non_negotiables.why: 200 km with 3,800m of climbing is a 7-10 hour day. Your nutrition strategy, pacing, and mental resilience need to be tested at this duration. The ninth hill comes at km 180+ — you need to know what your body and mind feel like after 8 hours of riding.

videos.title: GCN Italia alla 50ª Granfondo Nove Colli
videos.description:
Alan e Giorgio hanno partecipato alla Nove Colli, granfondo tra le più importanti giunta alla sua cinquantesima edizione. In questo video vedremo come se la sono cavata i nostri due presentatori...

Guarda su Komoot il percorso di Giorgio 👉  https://www.komoot.it/tour/518936696?ref=atd
ed Alan 👉  https://www.komoot.it/tour/518938779?ref=atd

Sottoscrivi l’abbonamento a GCN+ adesso: https://gcn.eu/plus

GCN Italia è il miglior canale di ciclismo su YouTube, da ora in italiano! Parleremo di ogni aspetto del ciclismo su strada… iscriviti e ti insegneremo come diventare un ciclista migliore grazie ai nostri suggerimenti. Ti aiuteremo a migliorare in salita, nella pedalata ed approfondiremo la tua conoscenza di questo sport meraviglioso. Potrai trovare inoltre le ultime notizie sul ciclismo italiano e mondiale proprio qui.

I nostri ciclisti ex-professionisti ti parleranno di tutto – allenamento, equipaggiamento, ciclismo amatoriale e gare pro!

Iscriviti subito e unisciti a noi sul miglior canale di ciclismo di YouTube in italiano.

videos.title: Nove Colli 2021 (205 km)
videos.description:
La mia prima esperienza alla Nove Colli risaliva al 2019; in quella edizione, decisi di fare (con un pò di rammarico) il percorso corto da 130 km per motivi meteo. Era rimasto un "conto in sospeso" con questa granfondo. Dovevo tornare e affrontare il percorso lungo da 205 km. Oggi eccomi nuovamente qui a Cesenatico per completare quella sfida.

Se apprezzi questo video e altri contenuti del mio canale,
fai una piccola donazione a "Giri in Bici" con Paypal:
